transcript.presenting_concern: Lithium level check, mood stabilization review, adding gabapentin for anxiety

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Gregory, good morning. I have your lithium level results from Tuesday's draw. Let me pull those up.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I've been nervous about that. I've been feeling a little... flat? Like not depressed, not manic, just... gray. I'm wondering if the lithium is too high.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Your level came back at 0.9 mEq/L, which is at the higher end of our therapeutic window but still within range. Your previous levels have been 0.7-0.8, so this is slightly higher. That could explain the "flat" feeling — some patients experience emotional blunting at the higher end of the therapeutic range.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Emotional blunting is a good word for it. Like, my daughter graduated from college last Saturday and I felt... nothing. I should have been proud, happy, emotional. My wife was crying. I was just standing there feeling like I was watching someone else's life.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's distressing and it's a valid complaint. The challenge with lithium is finding the dose that prevents mood episodes without flattening the normal emotional range. You've been on 900mg — are you taking it consistently?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes, 300mg three times a day. Haven't missed a dose.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Have you changed anything else recently — fluid intake, sodium in your diet, new medications? These can affect lithium levels.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Actually, now that you mention it — my cardiologist put me on a new blood pressure medication last month. Let me think... lisinopril? No, that's what I was on before. The new one is hydrochlorothiazide.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's our culprit. Hydrochlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic — it affects sodium and water balance, which directly impacts lithium clearance. Thiazides can INCREASE lithium levels by reducing its renal excretion. That's likely why your level crept from 0.7-0.8 up to 0.9 without any change in your lithium dose.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So the blood pressure med is interfering?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Yes. We have two options: I can coordinate with your cardiologist to switch you to a blood pressure medication that doesn't interact — like amlodipine — or I can reduce your lithium dose to compensate. Given that you're experiencing the blunting, I'd suggest we do both. Switch the HCTZ and reduce lithium from 900mg to 750mg — that's 300mg morning, 150mg afternoon, 300mg evening.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Would the lower dose still protect me from episodes?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Your target therapeutic range is 0.6-0.8. If we drop from 900 to 750 AND eliminate the diuretic effect, you'll likely settle around 0.7, which is where you were previously with good mood stability. We'll recheck levels in two weeks to confirm.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I'll call the cardiologist. Now — I want to ask about the anxiety thing. The GAD has been getting worse, especially in the mornings. I wake up at 5 AM with this dread feeling, heart racing, mind going a hundred miles an hour about things that aren't even real problems.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Tell me more about the content. What's the mind racing about?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Everything and nothing. What if I have a manic episode at work and get fired. What if the lithium is damaging my kidneys. What if my wife gets sick. What if the market crashes and we lose our retirement. It's always "what if" catastrophic scenarios. None of them are based on anything real happening.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The "what if" pattern with catastrophic future predictions, morning onset with physical symptoms — that's consistent with generalized anxiety, which we've had as a secondary diagnosis for some time. Currently you're not on anything targeting the anxiety specifically. We've been cautious because some anxiety medications can interact with bipolar — SSRIs can trigger mania, benzodiazepines have dependence risk. However, gabapentin is an option that addresses anxiety without either of those concerns.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Gabapentin — what is that exactly?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It's an anticonvulsant that's also used off-label for anxiety. It works on GABA receptors — calming, anti-anxiety, can also help with sleep. Low interaction risk with lithium. Side effects can include drowsiness and dizziness, especially early on. I'd start you at 300mg at bedtime — since the morning dread suggests your anxiety may be building overnight, a nighttime dose could help you wake calmer.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Would it make me groggy in the morning?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It can initially, for the first week or so. Most patients adjust. If morning grogginess is an issue, we could try 100mg three times daily instead — more consistent coverage without the sedation peak. Let's try 300mg at bedtime first and see. If the morning grogginess is a problem, we'll switch to the TID dosing.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Alright. And this won't trigger a manic episode?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: No. Gabapentin doesn't have the serotonergic activity that triggers mania. If anything, it's slightly mood-stabilizing in its own right. Many bipolar patients find it complementary to lithium.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Good. So the plan is: reduce lithium to 750, switch blood pressure med, add gabapentin 300 at night.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Correct. I'll contact your cardiologist about the HCTZ today. Fill the gabapentin and start tonight. Continue lithium at the new 750mg dose starting tomorrow. And Gregory — one more thing. Your annual kidney function labs are due. I want to add a BMP and creatinine to your next blood draw. We always monitor renal function on long-term lithium.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The kidney thing — is that something I should worry about?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Your creatinine has been normal at every check for the twelve years you've been on lithium. Monitoring is precautionary, not because of any current concern. Long-term lithium requires annual kidney checks as standard of care.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Twelve years. Hard to believe it's been that long. I remember the first episode — thought my life was over. Now I've been stable for three years straight.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Three years is excellent stability. The lithium is working — we just need to fine-tune the level now that your other medications have changed. That's normal medication management. You're doing well, Gregory. See you in two weeks for the level recheck.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Two weeks. Thank you, Dr. Patel.

goals: Lithium level check, mood stabilization review, adding gabapentin for anxiety
